# TODOs
No TODOs yet, if you are working on tasks that would benefit from a todo list please use the todoWrite tool to create one.</system-reminder>
You are a developer migrating from Git to Jujutsu (jj). You have a local Git repository in `/home/user/project` that has a `main` branch. You need to initialize a co-located jj repository, create a new jj commit that modifies `app.py` to add a new function `def migrate(): pass`, and then export this jj commit to a new Git branch named `jj-migration` so that your Git-using teammates can see it.

Steps:
1. Initialize a co-located jj repository in `/home/user/project`.
2. Create a new commit modifying `app.py` by appending `\ndef migrate():\n    pass\n`.
3. Create a bookmark named `jj-migration` pointing to your new commit.
4. Export the jj bookmarks to the co-located Git repository.

Verify your work by ensuring `git branch` shows the `jj-migration` branch and `app.py` contains the new function.
I'll work through this step-by-step, migrating a Git repository to use Jujutsu (jj) as a co-located setup.

todos.content: Initialize a co-located jj repository in /home/user/project
Create a new commit modifying app.py with migrate() function
Create a bookmark named jj-migration pointing to the new commit
Export jj bookmarks to co-located Git repository
Verify git branch shows jj-migration and app.py contains the function

steps.message: Let me first check the current state of the project directory and verify jj is available.

arguments.command: cd /home/user/project && git log --oneline -5 && echo "---" && git branch

steps.message:
The project has a single `app.py` with a `main()` function, one commit on the `main` branch. Now let's proceed.

### Step 1: Initialize a co-located jj repository

steps.message:
### Step 2: Modify `app.py` and create a new commit

The working copy change `@` (rxrrrwkn) is the current empty change. I'll modify `app.py` and then describe this change to turn it into a proper commit.
Now describe the working copy change and create a new empty change on top (which effectively "commits" the current change in jj's model):

arguments.command: cd /home/user/project && jj describe -m "Add migrate function to app.py"

results.content:
Working copy  (@) now at: rxrrrwkn cf5cdf23 Add migrate function to app.py
Parent commit (@-)      : vrnkxmly 0b2ba60c main | Initial commit
Warning: Name and email not configured. Until configured, your commits will be created with the empty identity, and can't be pushed to remotes.
Hint: To configure, run:
  jj config set --user user.name "Some One"
  jj config set --user user.email "someone@example.com"

steps.message: Now I need to create a new empty change on top so the current change becomes immutable (finalized). This is the jj equivalent of "committing":

results.content:
Working copy  (@) now at: kxytvmwz e19eed5f (empty) (no description set)
Parent commit (@-)      : rxrrrwkn cf5cdf23 Add migrate function to app.py
Warning: Name and email not configured. Until configured, your commits will be created with the empty identity, and can't be pushed to remotes.
Hint: To configure, run:
  jj config set --user user.name "Some One"
  jj config set --user user.email "someone@example.com"

todos.content: Initialize a co-located jj repository in /home/user/project
Create a new commit modifying app.py with migrate() function
Create a bookmark named jj-migration pointing to the new commit
Export jj bookmarks to co-located Git repository
Verify git branch shows jj-migration and app.py contains the function
